preamble.summary: Psalm 62 is a meditation on waiting silently for God alone. The psalmist declares that his soul rests in silence before God, who is his rock, salvation, and fortress. Enemies plot to bring him down from his high position, using flattery and hidden curses. Yet twice the psalmist returns to the refrain of silent trust. He warns against trusting in oppression, robbery, or wealth, and closes with a double declaration: power belongs to God, and faithful love belongs to God, and God repays each person according to what they have done.
preamble.remarkable: The word ak ('surely, only, alone') appears six times in this psalm, giving it a distinctive theological rhythm. Each occurrence narrows the field of trust until God is the only thing left standing. The two refrains (verses 2-3 and 6-7) are nearly identical but with a crucial shift: in verse 2 'I will not be greatly shaken,' in verse 6 'I will not be shaken' — the qualifier is removed. Silence before God deepens into more complete confidence. The final two verses deliver the psalm's theology in concentrated form: God possesses both oz (power, strength) and chesed (faithful love). These are not in tension but held together in one being.
preamble.friction: The superscription assigns this to David with the notation al Yedutun, likely indicating a musical tradition or choir associated with Jeduthun (one of David's three chief musicians). The identity of the enemies in verses 4-5 is unspecified — they use blessing as a cover for cursing, which suggests political rather than military opposition. The meaning of 'silence' (dumiyyah) has been debated: is it passive waiting, active stillness, or a deliberate refusal to seek human help? The psalm seems to intend all three. The weighing metaphor in verse 10 (men are lighter than a breath on the scales) echoes wisdom traditions and anticipates the Egyptian imagery of judgment scales.
preamble.connections: The 'rock' and 'fortress' language connects to David's earlier experiences in the wilderness (1 Samuel 23-24, Psalm 18). The warning against trusting riches anticipates Jesus' teaching about wealth (Matthew 6:19-21, Luke 12:13-21). The declaration that God repays each person according to their deeds appears in Proverbs 24:12, Jeremiah 17:10, and Romans 2:6. Paul's quotation shows this psalm's theology reaching into the New Testament understanding of divine justice.

verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician, to Jeduthun, A Psalm of David.
verses.rendering: For the director of music. According to Jeduthun. A psalm of David.
translator_notes: Jeduthun (Yedutun) was one of three chief musicians appointed by David (1 Chronicles 25:1-3). The notation 'according to Jeduthun' likely indicates a musical style, tune, or performance tradition associated with his guild.

verses.text_hebrew: אַ֣ךְ אֶל־אֱ֭לֹהִים דּוּמִיָּ֣ה נַפְשִׁ֑י מִ֝מֶּ֗נּוּ יְשׁוּעָתִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Truly my soul waiteth upon God: from him cometh my salvation.
verses.rendering:
My soul waits in silence for God alone;
from him comes my deliverance.
verses.expanded_rendering: The opening word ak ('surely, only, alone') sets the theological agenda for the entire psalm — everything will be narrowed to God alone. The word dumiyyah ('silence, stillness, quiet waiting') describes not passivity but a deliberate refusal to seek rescue from any other source. The soul is not empty but focused, directed entirely toward God. Deliverance (yeshuah) is positioned as coming from God — the preposition mimmennu ('from him') marks God as the sole origin of rescue.
translator_notes: The word dumiyyah is related to the verb damam ('be silent, be still'). It appears in Psalm 65:2 as well: 'To you silence is praise.' Here it describes the inner posture of the soul before God — waiting without agitation, complaint, or frantic searching for alternatives.

key_terms.semantic_range: silence, stillness, quiet waiting, calm trust
key_terms.note: This is not the silence of absence but of focused attention — the soul is quiet because it has stopped looking elsewhere for help.

verses.text_hebrew: אַךְ־ה֣וּא צ֭וּרִי וִישׁוּעָתִ֑י מִ֝שְׂגַּבִּ֗י לֹא־אֶמּ֥וֹט רַבָּֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: He only is my rock and my salvation; he is my defence; I shall not be greatly moved.
verses.rendering:
He alone is my rock and my deliverance,
my high fortress — I will not be greatly shaken.
translator_notes: The qualifier rabbah ('greatly') is significant — the psalmist acknowledges he may be shaken but not overwhelmed. This honest admission is removed in the second refrain (verse 7), suggesting that the act of meditating on God's sufficiency has deepened the psalmist's confidence.

verses.text_hebrew: עַד־אָ֤נָה ׀ תְּהֽוֹתְת֣וּ עַל־אִ֭ישׁ תְּרָצְּח֣וּ כֻלְּכֶ֑ם כְּקִ֥יר נָ֝ט֗וּי גָּדֵ֥ר הַדְּחוּיָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: How long will ye imagine mischief against a man? ye shall be slain all of you: as a bowing wall shall ye be, and as a tottering fence.
verses.rendering:
How long will all of you attack a man?
You will be crushed —
all of you — like a leaning wall,
like a fence about to collapse.
translator_notes: The verb tehotetu ('you set upon, you rush against, you assault') describes a coordinated attack against a single person. The images of a leaning wall (qir natuy) and a tottering fence (gader ha-dechuyah) are applied to the attackers themselves — they think they are strong, but they are structurally unsound and about to fall.

verses.text_hebrew: אַ֤ךְ מִשְּׂאֵת֨וֹ ׀ יָעֲצ֣וּ לְהַדִּ֗יחַ יִרְצ֥וּ כָזָ֑ב בְּפִ֥יו יְ֝בָרֵ֗כוּ וּבְקִרְבָּ֥ם יְקַלְלוּ־סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: They only consult to cast him down from his excellency: they delight in lies: they bless with their mouth, but they curse inwardly. Selah.
verses.rendering:
They scheme only to bring him down from his position;
they take pleasure in deception.
With their mouths they bless,
but inside they curse. Selah.
translator_notes: The word mis-seto ('from his elevation, from his dignity') suggests the target holds a position of honor that the conspirators want to dismantle. The contrast between outward blessing and inward cursing (be-fiv yevarekhu u-ve-qirbam yeqalelu) describes political duplicity — flattery as a weapon.

verses.text_hebrew: אַ֣ךְ לֵ֭אלֹהִים דּ֣וֹמִּי נַפְשִׁ֑י כִּי־מִ֝מֶּ֗נּוּ תִּקְוָתִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: My soul, wait thou only upon God; for my expectation is from him.
verses.rendering:
My soul, wait in silence for God alone,
for my hope comes from him.
translator_notes: The second refrain shifts from declaration ('my soul waits') to self-command ('my soul, wait!'). The psalmist is preaching to himself, redirecting his own attention back to God after describing the threat of his enemies. The word tiqvah ('hope, expectation') replaces yeshuah ('deliverance') from verse 2, broadening the scope from immediate rescue to comprehensive expectation.

verses.text_hebrew: אַךְ־ה֣וּא צ֭וּרִי וִישׁוּעָתִ֑י מִ֝שְׂגַּבִּ֗י לֹ֣א אֶמּֽוֹט׃
verses.text_kjv: He only is my rock and my salvation: he is my defence; I shall not be moved.
verses.rendering:
He alone is my rock and my deliverance,
my high fortress — I will not be shaken.
translator_notes: The critical difference from verse 3: the word rabbah ('greatly') is removed. In verse 3, 'I will not be greatly shaken.' Here, 'I will not be shaken' — period. The qualifier has been stripped away. The act of silent waiting before God has produced deeper confidence.

verses.text_hebrew: עַל־אֱ֭לֹהִים יִשְׁעִ֣י וּכְבוֹדִ֑י צוּר־עֻ֝זִּ֗י מַחְסִ֥י בֵֽאלֹהִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: In God is my salvation and my glory: the rock of my strength, and my refuge, is in God.
verses.rendering:
On God rest my deliverance and my glory;
my mighty rock, my refuge, is in God.
translator_notes: The psalmist now claims that even his kavod ('glory, honor, weight') depends on God. This is a striking admission for someone under attack — his social standing, his reputation, his significance are not self-generated but God-given. The stacking of titles (rock of strength, refuge) emphasizes comprehensive dependence.

key_terms.note: The psalmist's glory is not self-made but rests 'on God' — his significance is derivative, dependent on divine sustaining.

verses.text_hebrew: בִּטְח֘וּ ב֤וֹ בְכׇל־עֵ֨ת ׀ עָ֗ם שִׁפְכוּ־לְפָנָ֥יו לְבַבְכֶ֑ם אֱלֹהִ֖ים מַחֲסֶה־לָ֣נוּ סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Trust in him at all times; ye people, pour out your heart before him: God is a refuge for us. Selah.
translator_notes: The psalm shifts from personal meditation to public exhortation. The verb shifkhu ('pour out') is vivid — it is the verb used for pouring out water or blood. The heart is to be emptied before God completely, holding nothing back. This communal instruction (am, 'people') transforms private trust into shared worship.

verses.text_hebrew: אַ֤ךְ ׀ הֶ֤בֶל בְּנֵֽי־אָדָ֗ם כָּזָ֥ב בְּנֵי־אִ֑ישׁ בְּ֝מֹאזְנַ֗יִם לַעֲל֥וֹת הֵ֣מָּה מֵהֶ֣בֶל יָֽחַד׃
verses.text_kjv: Surely men of low degree are vanity, and men of high degree are a lie: to be laid in the balance, they are altogether lighter than vanity.
verses.rendering:
Common people are only a breath;
important people are a lie.
Placed on scales, together they rise —
they weigh less than a breath.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm makes no distinction between social classes when it comes to reliability — bney adam ('sons of adam,' common people) are hevel (vapor, breath, nothing substantial), and bney ish ('sons of a man,' people of standing) are kazav (a lie, a deception). When both are placed on the scales of reality, they float upward together because they have no weight. The metaphor is devastating: the very people one might trust for protection or alliance are lighter than emptiness itself. Only God has substance.
translator_notes: The distinction between bney adam and bney ish is social — the first refers to ordinary people, the second to people of rank or importance. The scales (moznayim) are a weighing instrument associated with justice and evaluation. The verb la-alot ('to go up') means they rise on the scale because they are so light — less than hevel, less than breath.

key_terms.semantic_range: vapor, breath, mist, vanity, emptiness, transience
key_terms.note: The same word that defines the theme of Ecclesiastes. Here it measures the insubstantiality of human beings when weighed against God's solidity.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־תִּבְטְח֣וּ בְעֹשֶׁק֮ וּבְגָזֵ֪ל אַֽל־תֶּ֫הְבָּ֥לוּ חַ֤יִל ׀ כִּֽי־יָנ֑וּב אַל־תָּשִׁ֥יתוּ לֵֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: Trust not in oppression, and become not vain in robbery: if riches increase, set not your heart upon them.
verses.rendering:
Do not trust in extortion;
do not put empty hope in robbery.
If wealth increases,
do not set your heart on it.
translator_notes: Three prohibitions in sequence: do not trust oppression (osheq), do not find significance in robbery (gazel), and do not attach your heart to increasing wealth (chayil). The verb tehbalu ('become empty, become vain') shares the same root as hevel — trusting in robbery makes a person as insubstantial as the breath described in the previous verse.

verses.text_hebrew: אַחַ֤ת ׀ דִּבֶּ֬ר אֱלֹהִ֗ים שְׁ֭תַּיִם ז֣וּ שָׁמָ֑עְתִּי כִּ֥י עֹ֝֗ז לֵאלֹהִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: God hath spoken once; twice have I heard this; that power belongeth unto God.
translator_notes: The 'one... two' formula (achat... shtayim) is a Hebrew numerical pattern used in wisdom literature (Proverbs 30:15, 18, 21) to introduce a definitive statement. God's single utterance contains a double truth: power and faithful love. The division of the single speech into two heard truths happens in the listener's understanding — God speaks unity; we perceive aspects.

verses.text_hebrew: וּלְךָ֣ אֲ֭דֹנָי חָ֑סֶד כִּֽי־אַתָּ֨ה תְשַׁלֵּ֖ם לְאִ֣ישׁ כְּמַעֲשֵֽׂהוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Also unto thee, O Lord, belongeth mercy: for thou renderest to every man according to his work.
verses.rendering:
and that faithful love belongs to you, Lord,
for you repay each person
according to what they have done.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's final statement holds together what human thinking tends to separate: raw power (oz) and faithful love (chesed) belong to the same God. A God of power without love would be terrifying; a God of love without power would be irrelevant. The psalmist has heard both in a single divine utterance. And because God possesses both, he is able to repay each person according to their deeds — justice is not arbitrary but flows from a being who is simultaneously powerful enough to enforce it and loving enough to be trusted with it.
translator_notes: The word chesed ('faithful love') is the psalm's final key term, paired with oz ('power') from the previous verse. The verb teshallem ('you repay, you complete, you render') implies that God brings each person's actions to their appropriate conclusion. This is not cold retribution but the faithful outworking of a God who is both strong and loving.

key_terms.semantic_range: loyal love, covenant faithfulness, steadfast kindness, mercy
key_terms.note: The climactic term of the psalm. Faithful love is not softness — it is paired with power and linked to repaying each person according to their deeds. It is love that acts with justice.
